missions.name: Systems Checkout
missions.description: Sol 1. You're on Mars. Let's make sure the bus still works.
missions.briefing:
EDL telemetry confirms touchdown. Welcome to Mars, Operator.

Your vehicle is a refurbished MSL-class rover — original deployment circa 2012, decommissioned, pulled from mothball, rebuilt under the Consortium's Second Surface Initiative. She's old. She's been patched more times than the documentation admits. But the reactor is warm and you're upright, which puts you ahead of the last three drops.

Before we clear you for field ops, we need to verify the primary systems survived descent. Work through these in order — each step unlocks the next.

POWER: Boot the RTG using the power control on your HUD. The Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator is your only power source. Every instrument, every heater cycle, every transmission draws from it. Nothing works until it's online — and nothing works if it ever goes offline permanently. Boot it now.

PROFILE [0]: Press 0 to open your operator profile. This shows your archetype, active mission, and science points — the currency that keeps the Consortium funding your surface time. Zero science means zero justification for keeping the lights on. Know your numbers.

WHEELS [B]: Press B to open the mobility panel. WASD drives the rover. This panel shows wheel condition and drive status — six wheels, each one degradable. Mars eats wheels. Keep an eye on them.

AVIONICS: Drive at least 5 meters in any direction. We need to confirm your inputs are translating to actual movement and not just warming up a dead actuator.

HEATER [H]: Press H to open the thermal panel. Mars surface temperature can drop below –80°C. The heater kicks in automatically when it gets too cold — but it draws power. Every minute it runs is a minute your instruments aren't. You'll learn to watch the thermal gauge the way you watch your fuel.

LGA [R]: Press R to open the Low-Gain Antenna panel. This is your mailbox. Mission assignments arrive here, and when you've completed an objective and need to file results, you transmit from here too. If you're ever unsure what to do next — check the LGA.

Complete the sequence. Once all checks pass, we'll push your first field assignment.

missions.name: First Light (Position Fix)
missions.description: Three stakes in the dirt, three pings to prove you're where you say you are. Time to drive
missions.briefing:
Systems check is green. You're cleared for field ops.

First order of business: position fix. Your nav system has a best-guess coordinate from EDL — good enough to land, not good enough to bill instrument time against. The Consortium's insurance does not cover best estimates.

Three transponder stakes — Alpha, Beta, Gamma — were air-dropped ahead of your arrival. They're already in the ground. Drive to each one and your antenna will ping it automatically when you're close enough. No interaction required — just get there.

NAVIGATION: Open your terrain map [M] to see all three stakes marked on the surface. The compass ribbon at the top of your HUD also shows nearby points of interest — watch for the markers as you drive. You can visit the stakes in any order.

This is your first real traverse. The checkout asked for 5 meters. These stakes are further out. Watch your footing — the terrain isn't flat, and rocks that look small from a distance can stop a wheel. Take it steady. You've got time.

Three pings. Then we talk science.

missions.name: RTG — Maintenance
missions.description: The RTG took damage during descent. It's at 60% and falling. Fix it now.
missions.briefing:
The RTG was damaged during EDL. It is currently operating at 60% durability and degrading every sol. This is your power source. Your only power source. If it reaches zero, the mission is over: no instruments, no heater, no drive, no recovery.

Every instrument on this vehicle has a durability rating. It drops passively from exposure, faster from active use, and sharply from hazards: dust storms, radiation events, extreme cold, meteor impacts. As durability falls, performance falls with it. Readings slow down, outputs lose precision, power draw climbs. Instruments don't just break suddenly. They get worse. It is always in your interest to repair early.

Open the RTG panel and use the REPAIR function. Small repairs cost only welding wire. Bigger damage starts requiring components too, and the cost scales with severity. Another reason not to let things slide.

After repair, the RTG will not return to 100%. It will come back to around 98%. Next time, 96%. Durability damage is permanent: every repair leaves a scar on the ceiling, and that ceiling only goes down. This is not a bug in your readout. It's a 175-year-old machine. Manage it.

missions.name: REMS — Environmental Baseline
missions.description: You're blind to the weather. Fix that.
missions.briefing:
Right now you have no surface weather data. You can't see wind speed, temperature, pressure, UV, or dust conditions. If a storm rolls in, you won't know until it hits you. REMS fixes that.

Open the REMS panel and activate it. Once on, REMS runs passively in the background at about 1 watt of continuous draw. It monitors atmospheric conditions and feeds live readings to your HUD. More importantly: REMS is your early warning system. Dust storms on Mars don't announce themselves. REMS does. If it's off when a storm approaches, you get no warning and no time to prepare.

There's a second reason to keep it running. When REMS is active, it applies a quality bonus to any science collected near the rover. Spectra, drill samples, contact readings: they're all worth more when REMS can certify the atmospheric conditions at the time of collection. The Consortium won't accept uncertified results at full value.

Turn it on. Leave it on. The only reason to shut it off is a power emergency, and even then, think twice.

missions.name: Orbital Delivery: DAN Field Hardware
missions.description: The Consortium shipped DAN field equipment to your site. They want subsurface data. Go collect their investment.
missions.briefing:
The Consortium has sent supplementary hardware for your DAN instrument via orbital drop. DAN reads subsurface neutron signatures: water ice, volatile pockets, geological structure beneath the surface. The instrument on your rover does the sensing, but certain field operations require deployable hardware that the original vehicle was never built to carry. The Consortium wants that capability online. So they paid for a drop.

Orbital drops are how the Consortium extends rover operations beyond what shipped with the vehicle. Their satellites are in Mars orbit carrying equipment, components, buildable hardware: everything a refurbished rover from 2012 wasn't designed for. Drops are expensive. When one lands at your site, someone decided it was worth the cost.

This one arrived early and off-target. Some of the delivery satellites still report in imperial. Apparently that wasn't in the refurbishment budget. The pod landed about half a mile from your position. A waypoint marker is active on your map and compass. Drive to it and open the cargo pod to collect your DAN extractor unit.

Once you've collected the payload, you need to return to the rock outcrop. It's a long drive back. Open the RTG panel and fire overdrive: the reactor dumps its thermal reserve into the power bus, doubling your drive speed. While the sequence runs, every instrument on the vehicle goes offline, including comms. The cycle completes automatically. When power returns, keep driving.

You won't need the DAN extractor yet. But corporate don't want their expensive gear collecting Martian dust and losing durability.

missions.name: MastCam: Survey Baseline
missions.description: The rover has a camera system on the mast. It sees more than rock and sky. Activate it.
missions.briefing:
Until now, you've been watching the rover from the outside. Your view comes from a companion micro-drone: a small autonomous camera that follows the vehicle and transmits a third-person feed. It's efficient, saves RTG power, and gives you good situational awareness. But it can't do science.

MastCam can. When you activate MastCam, your feed switches to the rover's onboard camera system, mounted on the instrument mast. You'll see the world through the rover's own eyes: a processed survey overlay that strips the terrain to wireframe and highlights rocks in color by mineral type. Basalt, hematite, olivine, sulfate, mudstone: each one lights up differently. This is how you find targets worth investigating.

Use A/D to pan the mast, W/S to tilt, scroll to zoom. Press Q to cycle the rock filter: isolate a single mineral type or show everything at once. The HUD bar above shows your heading, elevation angle, and field of view.

When you see a rock worth tagging, aim at it and hold E. The scan takes a few seconds. When it completes, a marker appears above the rock confirming it's in your survey registry. Tagged rocks produce better science and better yields from every instrument that touches them. You can work with untagged rocks, but you'll get less from them.

MastCam has a survey range. Rocks outside it won't appear in the overlay. If you're not seeing anything, drive closer or zoom in. Tag at least three rocks to complete this baseline.

missions.name: ChemCam — LIBS Verification
missions.description: There's a laser on the mast that vaporizes rock and reads the plasma. Point it at something. For Science.
missions.briefing:
ChemCam is a laser spectrometer mounted on the same mast as MastCam. It fires a focused infrared pulse at a rock target, vaporizes a pinpoint of surface material, and reads the plasma emission to identify elemental composition. No contact required. Same controls as MastCam: A/D pan, W/S tilt, scroll to zoom.

ChemCam only fires on rocks you've already tagged with MastCam. Aim at a tagged target and hold E to begin the LIBS pulse train. Keep holding. The laser fires for several seconds, then the spectrometer integrates the signal. If you release E during the pulse, the shot aborts and your round is refunded. Once integration completes, a spectrum readout appears. Press SEE RESULTS to review it.

You have 10 shots before the laser needs to cool down. Each shot also draws heavy power. Pick your targets: not every rock is worth a shot. The rarer the mineral, the more valuable the spectrum.

One more thing. ChemCam's spectral accuracy improves as you accumulate science points. Early spectra will show fewer labeled peaks. As your SP grows, the calibration curve fills in and your readings get sharper. The instrument gets better the more science you do. Log one spectrum to complete this verification.

missions.name: APXS — Contact Science
missions.description: Alpha particles in, X-rays out. Press the sensor against a rock and read what fluoresces.
missions.briefing:
APXS is an Alpha Particle X-Ray Spectrometer. It bombards a rock surface with alpha particles. When those particles hit atoms in the rock, the atoms fluoresce: they emit X-ray photons at energies unique to each element. Iron fluoresces differently from silicon, calcium differently from sulfur. Catch those photons with a detector and you get a full elemental composition by percentage.

The physics hasn't changed in 175 years. Alpha decay is alpha decay. A sensor head pressed against Martian rock in 2187 reads the same fluorescence it would have in 2012. That's why this instrument survived the refurbishment: it doesn't need firmware updates. It needs contact.

APXS is mounted on the rover's robotic arm. This is your first time using the arm: A/D to swing, W/S to extend. Position the sensor head against a rock and press E to begin the contact pass. The turret rotates automatically to bring APXS forward. Do not move the rover while the analysis is running.

When the pass begins, your display shows the analysis in real time. Photons scatter outward from the rock surface, each one color-coded by element. Move your detector to intercept them. The spectrum panel at the bottom tracks your measured composition against the true values. The closer the match, the better your accuracy. The source has limited power: catch what matters before it runs out.

Pay attention to what APXS tells you about a rock's makeup. Carbonate and sulfate enrichment on the surface can indicate what's happening beneath it. Rock composition is not just science for the archive. It's information about the ground you're standing on.

missions.name: Drill — Sample + Power Recovery
missions.description: Full science pipeline complete on target rock. Laser drill armed. Extract the core.
missions.briefing:
You've tagged this rock with MastCam, analyzed its spectrum with ChemCam, and read its surface composition with APXS. You know what it's made of. Now extract the core.

The drill is mounted on the rover's robotic arm. Use A/D to swing the arm and W/S to extend it. A target dot appears on any rock in range: green means you can collect, red means your inventory is full. Aim the drill head at a rock and hold E to fire. It's a laser drill: sustained thermal ablation, not mechanical boring. Keep holding until the progress bar completes. Release early and the cycle cancels. When the sample is collected, the rock is consumed.

You can drill any rock, tagged or not. But the science you've already done on it matters. MastCam-tagged rocks drill faster. ChemCam and APXS analysis increase the sample weight and drop bonus trace elements. A rock that's been through the full pipeline: tag, spectrum, contact scan, then drill, gives you dramatically more than one you just walked up to and cut open. That's the workflow.

Drilling is the heaviest power draw on the vehicle. After your first core, your battery will be low. Open the RTG panel and activate the power shunt. The shunt dumps reserve energy directly into the battery, refilling it. The tradeoff: while the shunt runs, your drive system disengages. You cannot move. Wait for the cycle to complete, then carry on.

Use the shunt whenever power is critical and you can afford to sit still.

missions.name: DAN — Subsurface Baseline
missions.description: Neutrons go down. Hydrogen slows them. Count what bounces back. Find the water.
missions.briefing:
DAN is a Dynamic Albedo of Neutrons detector. It fires high-energy neutrons straight down through the rover chassis into the ground. When those neutrons hit hydrogen atoms in the subsurface, they slow down: hydrogen thermalizes neutrons in a way nothing else does. DAN measures the return signal and counts what bounced back. A spike in thermalized neutron return means hydrogen. On Mars, hydrogen means water ice.

The original Curiosity carried this detector to measure hydrogen in the top meter of soil. The physics hasn't changed in 175 years and neither has the instrument. DAN finds the water. What you deploy at the site afterward is what's new: the extraction hardware from your orbital drop uses nanobot extractors to mine subsurface ice in small quantities. The rover detects. The buildable extracts.

Open the DAN panel with [5] and activate it. DAN runs passively in the background at 10 watts while you drive. Watch the signal readout. When the return count spikes, stop immediately. Driving away from a hydrogen signal resets the integration and you'll lose it.

When DAN detects a hit, you'll receive a notification. Press [5] to open the DAN panel and click the PROSPECT button while still inside the detection area. Once prospecting begins, the rover cannot move for approximately two Mars hours. The detector needs stability to resolve the signal at depth. Stay still and let it work.

If water is confirmed, the system will ask if you want to deploy your DAN extractor at the site. Deploying consumes the unit from your inventory. Check the reservoir quality reading before you commit. Not every water source is worth the hardware. A weak reservoir might not justify spending an extractor you could use on a richer site later. If the signal is inconclusive, the data still matters: each inconclusive reading refines DAN's calibration, making the next detection more likely.

objectives.label: Drive until DAN registers a signal

missions.name: SAM — Lab Checkout
missions.description: The onboard lab can heat a rock sample until it talks. Load one and run the oven.
missions.briefing:
SAM is the Sample Analysis at Mars instrument suite: gas chromatograph, mass spectrometer, and tunable laser spectrometer, all packed into the rover's belly. It is the most complex analytical tool on the vehicle. Everything else tells you what a rock looks like or what it's made of. SAM tells you what happened to it, what lived in it, and what it's hiding.

SAM consumes drill samples from your inventory. Open the SAM panel, load a sample, and select an experiment mode. Right now you have access to pyrolysis: the oven heats your sample in stages, and volatiles evaporate at different temperature thresholds. Higher temperatures release more tightly bound molecules. As you accumulate science points, additional modes will unlock: wet chemistry and isotope analysis. Each mode asks a different question and produces different results. For now, pyrolysis is your lab.

When the experiment begins, you're operating the oven directly. Your mouse controls the temperature. Four phases run in sequence, each with a target band that climbs higher. Keep the temperature trace inside the green zone. Quality drains when you drift outside it. If quality hits zero, the experiment fails and the sample is lost. Precision matters.

When the experiment completes, SAM reports a discovery and may produce byproducts: refined materials, trace elements, compounds. These are not waste. Collect everything. What SAM produces will matter more than you think.

Different rocks yield different results. Pay attention to which samples give you the best discoveries. Not every rock is worth an experiment. Run one full cycle to complete this checkout.

missions.name: RAD: Ground-Level Survey
missions.description: The Consortium needs radiation readings at ground level. There's a known hotspot in your area. Drive to it.
missions.briefing:
RAD is a particle telescope flush with the rover deck, pointing skyward. It counts incoming radiation by species: protons, neutrons, gamma rays, heavy ions. Mars has no magnetosphere: no global magnetic field, no shielding. Everything the sun throws at this planet reaches the surface. The Consortium has orbital radiation surveys of your area, but orbital data doesn't tell them what it's like on the ground. They're planning crewed surface operations and they need ground-truth dosimetry from an actual vehicle at actual elevation. That's you.

Open the RAD panel and activate it. It runs passively at 2 watts. The HUD shows your current zone classification, dose rate, cumulative sol dose, and particle count rate.

A known radiation hotspot in your area has been flagged from orbital survey data. A waypoint marker is active on your map. Drive to it. As you enter higher-radiation terrain, your camera feed will start to glitch and your instruments will degrade. In hazardous zones, all science instruments go offline entirely. Only RAD and the RTG keep working. RAD shows you the direction and distance to the nearest safe zone so you always know which way is out.

While inside the radiation zone, RAD will detect a radiation event: a transient spike from solar activity or cosmic ray interaction. When the alert fires, choose DECODE. The decode minigame captures incoming particles by species. Catch rate and composition determine the event classification and quality of your data. When decoding completes, review the results in the science archive to log the experiment.

Once the data is logged, get out. A return waypoint will mark safe ground. Drive to it. When you arrive, check your instruments: radiation degrades durability. Open any damaged instrument panel and run a REPAIR. The Consortium gets their ground-level survey. You get science points, repair practice under real conditions, and a better understanding of where on this map you can work and where you can't.

missions.name: UHF — First Uplink
missions.description: Science on the rover is meaningless until Earth acknowledges receipt. Queue it. Send it. Wait for the window.
missions.briefing:
Every experiment you've run, every spectrum, every prospect, every contact analysis: it's all sitting on the rover. Locally stored. Locally meaningless. Science does not exist in the Consortium's ledger until Earth acknowledges receipt. Until then, it's unverified data on a 175-year-old hard drive 225 million kilometers from anyone who can read it.

The UHF antenna is your uplink. It transmits queued science data to orbital relay satellites during scheduled pass windows. No relay overhead means no transmission. You wait for the window, you send what you have, and you hope the acknowledgment comes back clean.

Before you transmit, you need to queue your data. Open the science log and review what you've collected. You have results from five instruments. Visit each section: ChemCam, APXS, SAM, DAN, and RAD. Queue at least one result from each for transmission.

Once your queue is loaded, open the UHF panel with [T] and make sure the antenna is enabled. When the next orbital pass begins, transmission starts automatically from the top of the queue. Each item takes bandwidth. Rarer discoveries take longer to transmit. If the pass window closes before the queue is empty, the remaining items wait for the next orbit.

When Earth acknowledges receipt, the science points become official. That number in your profile stops being a local estimate and starts being your record. This is how you earn your keep.

missions.name: LGA — Deep Signal
missions.description: Your patron thinks you've earned something. A firmware upgrade just dropped from orbit. What it picks up is not in any briefing.
missions.briefing:
Your patron has authorized a firmware upgrade for the Low-Gain Antenna. An orbital drop containing a DSN Archaeology Module is inbound to a cache site on your map. This is not standard Consortium equipment. Your patron pulled strings and spent political capital to get this approved. Whatever you've been doing out here, someone noticed.

The module extends your LGA receiver into deprecated frequency bands: legacy Deep Space Network channels that haven't been officially active since the last century of Mars operations. But not active doesn't mean silent. The Deep Space Network is a mesh of relay stations across the inner solar system. Transmissions that entered the network decades or centuries ago are still bouncing between nodes, reflecting off relay mirrors, cycling through the system in degraded loops. They never stop. They just get quieter.

With this module installed and enough processing power, your rover's onboard ML can isolate those remnant waveforms and reconstruct the original transmissions. Voice logs. Station reports. Personal messages. Signals from people who were here before you, preserved in the noise of a network that never forgets.

Drive to the cache marked on your map and retrieve the module. Then open the LGA panel with [R] and install it. This is your first instrument upgrade: a permanent enhancement that improves what the hardware can do. Future upgrades for other instruments will work the same way.

Once installed, make sure both LGA and UHF are active. LGA listens for the signal. UHF provides the relay bandwidth for reconstruction. Both antennas need to be on. When a transmission is successfully reconstructed, it will appear in the DSN Archive. Open it. Read what came through. Some of them have audio.
